original: Feoh byþ frofur fira gehwylcum; / sceal þeah manna gehwylc miclun hit dælan / gif he wile for drihtne domes hleotan.

translations.standard: Wealth is a comfort to all men; / yet must every man bestow it freely, / if he wish to gain glory in the sight of the Lord.
translations.literal: Wealth is comfort [to] men each; / shall though men each greatly it deal [share] / if he will before Lord glory obtain.

original: Ur byþ anmod ond oferhyrned, / felafrecne deor, feohteþ mid hornum / mære morstapa; þæt is modig wuht.

translations.standard: The aurochs is proud and has great horns; / it is a very savage beast and fights with its horns; / a great ranger of the moors, it is a creature of mettle.
translations.literal: Aurochs is single-minded and over-horned, / very-daring beast, fights with horns / famous moor-stepper; that is brave creature.

original: Ðorn byþ ðearle scearp; ðegna gehwylcum / anfeng ys yfyl, ungemetum reþe / manna gehwylcum, ðe him mid resteð.

translations.standard: The thorn is exceedingly sharp; an evil thing to grip / for any knight, uncommonly severe / to all who rest among them.
translations.literal: Thorn is severely sharp; thanes each / grasping is evil, immeasurably fierce / men each, who him with rests.

original: Os byþ ordfruma ælere spræce, / wisdomes wraþu ond witena frofur, / ond eorla gehwam eadnys ond tohiht.

translations.standard: The mouth is the source of all language, / a pillar of wisdom and a comfort to wise men, / a blessing and a joy to every knight.
translations.literal: Mouth is source [of] every speech, / wisdom's support and wise-men's comfort, / and earls each prosperity and hope.

original: Rad byþ on recede rinca gehwylcum / sefte, ond swiþhwæt, ðam ðe sitteþ on ufan / meare mægenheardum ofer milpaþas.

translations.standard: Riding seems soft to every warrior while he is indoors / and very courageous to him who bestrides / a stout steed over the mile-paths.
translations.literal: Riding is in hall warriors each / easy, and very-vigorous, [to] that who sits on above / horse power-hard over mile-paths.

original: Cen byþ cwicera gehwam, cuþ on fyre / blac ond beorhtlic, byrneþ oftust / ðær hi æþelingas inne restaþ.

translations.standard: The torch is known to every living man by its pale, bright flame; it always burns where princes rest within.
translations.literal: Torch is [to] living each, known by fire / pale and bright-like, burns oftenest / where they princes inside rest.

original: Gyfu gumena byþ gleng ond herenys, / wraþu ond wyrþscype ond wræcna gehwam / ar ond ætist, ðe byþ oþra leas.

translations.standard: Generosity is a credit and honor to men, and support and dignity to every homeless man who is otherwise destitute.
translations.literal: Gift [of] men is grace and praise, / support and worship and exiles each / help and subsistence, who is others-less.

original: Wenne bruceþ, ðe can weana lyt / sares ond sorge ond him sylfa hæfþ / blæd ond blysse ond eac byrga geniht.

translations.standard: Bliss is enjoyed by him who knows little of woe, pain or sorrow, and has prosperity and happiness and a good enough town.
translations.literal: Joy enjoys, who knows woes little / sore and sorrow and him self has / prosperity and bliss and also cities [strongholds] sufficiency.

original: Hægl byþ hwitust corna; hwyrft þæt of heofones lyfte, / wealcaþ hit windes scura; weorþeþ hit to wætere syððan.

translations.standard: Hail is the whitest of grains; it is whirled from the vault of heaven and is tossed about by gusts of wind and then it becomes water.
translations.literal: Hail is whitest [of] grains; turns that from heaven's air, / tosses it wind's showers; becomes it to water afterwards.

original: Nyd byþ nearu on breostan; weorþeþ hi þeah oft niþa bearnum / to helpe ond to hæle gehwæþre, gif hi his hlystaþ æror.

translations.standard: Trouble is oppressive to the heart; yet often it proves a source of help and salvation to the children of men, if they heed it in time.
translations.literal: Need is narrow [oppressive] on breast; becomes it though often men's children / to help and to health each, if they it listen-to before.

original: Is byþ ofereald, ungemetum slidor, / glisniaþ glæshluttur gimmum gelicust, / flor forste geworuht, fæger ansyne.

translations.standard: Ice is very cold and immeasurably slippery; it glistens as clear as glass and most like to gems; it is a floor wrought by the frost, fair to look upon.
translations.literal: Ice is over-old, immeasurably slippery, / glistens glass-clear gems likest, / floor by-frost wrought, fair [in] appearance.

original: Ger byþ gumena hiht, ðon God læteþ, / halig heofones cyning, hrusan syllan / beorhte bleda beornum ond ðearfum.

translations.standard: Summer is a joy to men, when God, the holy King of Heaven, suffers the earth to bring forth shining fruits for rich and poor alike.
translations.literal: Year [Harvest] is men's hope, when God lets, / holy heaven's king, earth give / bright fruits [to] rich and needy.

original: Eoh byþ utan unsmþe treow, / heard hrusan fæst, hyrde fyres, / wyrtrumun underwreþyd, wyn on eþle.

translations.standard: The yew is a tree with rough bark, hard and fast in the earth, supported by its roots, a guardian of flame and a joy upon the estate.
translations.literal: Yew is outside rough tree, / hard earth-fast, shepherd [of] fire, / by-roots under-propped, joy on homeland.

original: Peorð byþ symble plega ond hleahtor / wlancum [ðær] wigan sittaþ on beorsele / bliþe ætsomne.

translations.standard: Peorth is a source of recreation and amusement to the great, where warriors sit blissfully together in the banqueting hall.
translations.literal: Peorth [Game-piece?] is always play and laughter / to-proud-ones [where] warriors sit in beer-hall / blithe together.

original: Eolh-secg eard hæfþ oftust on fenne / wexeþ on wæture, wundaþ grimme, / blode breneþ beorna gehwylcne / ðe him ænige onfeng gedeþ.

translations.standard: The elk-sedge is usually found in a marsh; it grows in the water and burns the blood, wounding any man who tries to grasp it.
translations.literal: Elk-sedge home has oftenest in fen / grows in water, wounds grimly, / [with] blood stains men each / who him any grasp makes.

original: Sigel semannum symble biþ on hihte, / ðonne hi hine feriaþ ofer fisces eþel, / oþ hi brimhengest bryngan to lande.

translations.standard: The sun is always a hope to seamen when they steer their ship over the fishes' bath, until the brine-steed brings them to land.
translations.literal: Sun [to] seamen always is in hope, / when they him [it] lead over fish's homeland, / until sea-stallion brings [them] to land.

original: Tir biþ tacna gehwylc, healdeþ trywa wel / wiþ æþelingas; a biþ on færylde / ofer nihta genipu, næfre swiceþ.

translations.standard: Tir is a star; it keeps faith well with princes; it is always on its course over the mists of night and never fails.
translations.literal: Tir is signs each, holds faith well / with princes; always is on journey / over night's mists, never deceives.

original: Beorc byþ bleda leas, bereþ efne swa ðeah / tanas butan tudder, biþ on telgum wliteg, / heah on helme hrysted fægere.

translations.standard: The birch bears no fruit, yet it has suckers without seed; it is beautiful in its branches, laden with leaves and touching the sky.
translations.literal: Birch is fruit-less, bears even so though / twigs without seed, is on branches beautiful, / high on crown adorned fairly.

original: Eh byþ æþelinga wyn, ðer hi æþelingas / ofer mearummodigum meðelstede fandaþ; / biþ on wicgum welegum wraþu gehwylcum.

translations.standard: The horse is a joy to princes in the presence of warriors, a steed in the pride of its hoofs, when rich men on horseback bandy words about it.
translations.literal: Horse is princes' joy, where they noble-ones / over steeds-spirited meeting-place test; / is on horses wealthy [to] support each.

original: Mann byþ on myrgþe his magan leof: / sceal þeah anra gehwylc oðrum swican, / forðum drihten wile dome sine / þæt earme flæsc eorþan betæcan.

translations.standard: The joyous man is dear to his kinsmen; yet every man is doomed to fail his fellow, for the Lord by His decree will commit the vile carrion to the earth.
translations.literal: Man is in mirth his kinsman dear: / shall though each-one [from] other depart, / because lord will [by] decree his / that wretched flesh [to] earth commit.

original: Lagu byþ leodum langsum þuhte, / gif hi sculun neþan on nacan tealtum / ond hi lages wealca þearle bregaþ / ond se brimhengest bridles ne gime.

translations.standard: The ocean seems interminable to men, if they must venture on the unsteady bark and the waves of the sea terrify them and the brine-steed heeds not its bridle.
translations.literal: Sea is [to] people long-seeming thought, / if they shall venture on boat unsteady / and them sea's waves greatly terrify / and the sea-stallion [of] bridle not heeds.

original: Ing wæs ærest mid East-Denum / gesewen secgun, oþ he siððan east / ofer wæg gewat; wæn æfter ran; / þus Heardingas þone hæle nemdun.

translations.standard: Ing was first seen by men among the East-Danes, till, followed by his chariot, he departed eastwards over the waves. So the Heardingas named the hero.
translations.literal: Ing was first with East-Danes / seen [by] men, until he afterwards east / over wave departed; wagon after ran; / thus Heardingas that hero named.

original: Eþel byþ oferleof æghwylcum men, / gif he mot ðær rihtes ond gerysena on / brucan on bolde bleadum oftast.

translations.standard: An estate is very dear to every man, if he can enjoy there in his house whatever is right and proper in constant prosperity.
translations.literal: Homeland [Estate] is over-dear [to] each man, / if he may there right and decencies on / enjoy in dwelling prosperity oftenest.

original: Dæg byþ drihtnes sond, deore metodes lyeht, / wyn ond wears dome eadgum ond earmum, / eallum to lyehte.

translations.standard: Day, the glorious light of the Creator, is sent by the Lord; it is a satisfaction and hope to rich and poor alike, and is of use to all.
translations.literal: Day is Lord's messenger, dear Creator's light, / joy and hope [by] decree [to] blessed and poor, / all to light.

original: Æsc byþ oferheah, eldum dyre / stiþ on staþule, stede rihte hylt, / ðeah him feohtan on firas monige.

translations.standard: The ash is exceedingly tall and precious to men. With its sturdy trunk it offers a stubborn resistance, though many men attack it.
translations.literal: Ash is over-high, [to] men dear / stiff on foundation, place rightly holds, / though him fight on men many.

original: Ac byþ on eorþan elda barnum / flæsces fodor, fereþ gelome / ofer ganotes bæþ; garsecg fandaþ / hwæþer ac hæbbe æþele treowe.

translations.standard: The oak fattens the flesh for the children of men; it often traverses the gannet's bath, and the ocean proves whether the oak keeps noble faith.
translations.literal: Oak is on earth [for] men's children / flesh's fodder, fares [travels] frequently / over gannet's bath [sea]; ocean tests / whether oak has noble faith.

original: Æsc byþ oferheah, eldum dyre / stiþ on staþule, stede rihte hylt, / ðeah him feohtan on firas monige.

translations.standard: The ash is exceedingly tall and precious to men. With its sturdy trunk it offers a stubborn resistance, though many men attack it.
translations.literal: Ash is over-high, [to] men dear / stiff on foundation, place rightly holds, / though him fight on men many.

original: Yr byþ æþelinga ond eorla gehwæs / wyn ond wyrþmynd, byþ on wicge fæger, / fæstlic on færelde, fyrdgeatewa sum.

translations.standard: The bow is a source of joy and honor to every prince and knight; it looks well on a horse and is a reliable piece of military equipment.
translations.literal: Bow [or Axe?] is princes' and earls' each / joy and honor, is on horse fair, / steadfast on journey, war-gear one.

original: Ior byþ ea-fisc ond þeah a bruceþ / fodres on foldan, hafaþ fægerne eard / wætre beworpen, ðær he wynnum leofaþ.

translations.standard: The eel is a river-fish and yet it always feeds on land; it has a fair home surrounded by water, where it lives in joy.
translations.literal: Eel [or Beaver?] is river-fish and though always enjoys / fodder on land, has fair home / [by] water surrounded, where he joyfully lives.

original: Ear byþ egle eorla gehwylcun, / ðonn[e] fæstlice flæsc onginneþ, / hraw colian, hrusan ceosan / blac to gebeddan; bleda gedreosaþ, / wynna gewitaþ, wera geswicaþ.

translations.standard: The grave is horrible to every knight, when the corpse begins to grow cold and is laid in the bosom of the dark earth; prosperity declines, happiness passes away and covenants are broken.
translations.literal: Grave [Dust/Earth] is hateful [to] earls each, / when fastly flesh begins, / corpse to cool, earth to-choose / pale as bedfellow; fruits fall-away, / joys depart, men's-covenants fail.
